stem: Virtual memory is_______.

options.label: A
options.text: memory on the hard disk that the CPU uses an extended RAM

options.label: C
options.text: only necessary if you do not have any RAM in your computer

options.label: D
options.text: a backup device for floppy disk

explanation: Virtual memory reserves an area of the hard disk (the page or swap file) and lets the OS move pages between it and RAM, so programs can address more memory than is physically installed. Option B is the trap: the whole point of virtual memory is that the backing store is on disk, not in RAM.

stem: USB stands for-
explanation: USB stands for Universal Serial Bus. It is 'serial' because data is sent one bit at a time over a differential pair, and 'universal' because a single connector standard replaced the older keyboard, mouse, serial, and parallel ports.

stem: In a typical memory experiment, participants are asked to recall stimuli in which phase?
explanation: Off-syllabus psychology trivia: a memory experiment has a presentation (study) phase where stimuli are shown and a test phase where retrieval is measured, so recall is elicited in the test phase. 'Material' and 'distinction' are not phase names at all.

stem: Participants are NOT told that they will be tested on a list of words presented in an experiment. This provides evidence of
explanation: Incidental learning is learning that happens without any intent to learn, so not warning participants about the later test is the defining setup. Intentional learning is the opposite condition (participants are told they will be tested).

stem: A test where participants are asked to retrieve in any order stimuli previously presented is
explanation: Free recall means producing the studied items in any order with no cues provided. Cued recall would supply a hint at test, and implicit/explicit memory describe the type of memory, not the test format.

stem: Participants take part in an experiment where they learn a number of words and are told that they will be tested later on what they have learned. This is a test of
explanation: Telling participants they will be tested and then asking them to deliberately retrieve the list makes it a conscious, intentional retrieval, which is the definition of an explicit memory test. Recognition is a trap: it is one specific explicit test format, but the stem never says the words are re-presented for a yes/no judgement.

stem: Storage is

options.label: A
options.text: the stage of memory where information is interpreted and transferred

options.label: B
options.text: the stage of memory where information is committed

options.label: C
options.text: the stage of memory most affected by direct manipulation in experiments

options.label: D
options.text: the stage of memory between presentation of stimuli and before test

explanation: Memory has three stages: encoding (interpreting and committing information), storage (retaining it), and retrieval (getting it back). Storage is therefore the retention interval between presentation of the stimuli and the test; options A and B describe encoding.

stem: Investigating what factors can affect memory storage can be done by

options.label: A
options.text: manipulating what happens before the test phase

options.label: B
options.text: informing people that they will be tested

options.label: D
options.text: manipulating what happens at the presentation phase

explanation: To isolate storage you must vary only what happens during the retention interval, that is, after presentation and before the test. Manipulating the presentation phase (D) targets encoding, and providing recall cues (C) targets retrieval.

stem: Which is NOT typically used to test memory retrieval?
explanation: The dot probe task measures attentional bias toward emotional stimuli; it is not a memory test at all. Word stem completion (implicit), cued recall (explicit) and serial position effects are all standard retrieval measures.

stem: With immediate testing, recall accuracy is poorest for items that occur in which position of a list?
explanation: The serial position curve is U-shaped: early items benefit from primacy (rehearsal into long-term memory) and late items from recency (still in short-term store), so middle items get neither boost and are recalled worst.

stem: Immediately testing recall can lead to greater recall for words at the end of a list. This is
explanation: Better recall of the last items on an immediate test is the recency effect. Serial position effect is the trap: it is the umbrella term for the whole U-shaped curve (primacy plus recency), not the end-of-list advantage specifically.

stem: The recency effect in memory retrieval can be reduced when

options.label: B
options.text: short- to long-term memory interference is introduced

options.label: C
options.text: a backwards masking task is used

explanation: Recency depends on the final items still sitting in the short-term store, so lengthening the retention interval (for example by inserting a filled delay before the test) wipes it out. Preventing rehearsal mainly attacks primacy, not recency.

stem: Which authors proposed the modal model of memory?
explanation: The modal (multi-store) model of sensory store to short-term store to long-term store is Atkinson and Shiffrin (1968). Craik and Lockhart (1972) proposed levels of processing, and Tulving and Thomson proposed encoding specificity.

stem: The modal model of memory was challenged on the grounds that

options.label: A
options.text: impaired short-term memory does not disrupt long-term memory

options.label: B
options.text: Short-term memory has a limited capacity

options.label: C
options.text: longer time in short-term memory predicts likelihood of long-term memory

options.label: D
options.text: long-term memory has a limited capacity

explanation: The modal model routes everything through short-term memory into long-term memory, so it predicts that damaging short-term memory should block new long-term learning. Neuropsychological cases with badly impaired short-term memory but intact long-term learning break that serial chain. Options B and D are not challenges: limited short-term capacity is a claim the model itself makes.

stem: According to levels of processing, which of the following leads to the deepest level of memory?
explanation: In Craik and Lockhart's levels of processing, depth increases from structural to phonological to semantic, so processing meaning produces the most durable trace. Perceptual processing is the shallowest level.

stem: A critical issue with the ‘levels of processing’ account of memory is

options.label: A
options.text: the distinction between perceptual and semantic processing

options.label: B
options.text: it is not compatible with the modal model

options.label: D
options.text: it does not explain how information is retrieved

explanation: The standard criticism is circularity: depth of processing is defined by how well the material is later remembered, and there is no independent measure of depth, so the theory cannot be falsified.

stem: Retrieval cues can improve recall because of
explanation: A cue helps only to the extent that the information it carries was part of the trace laid down at encoding, that is, overlap between the cue and what was encoded (encoding specificity). Interference and decay explain forgetting, not why cues help.

stem: ‘Specific operations […] determine what is stored, and what is stored determines what retrieval cues are effective’ refers to what term?
explanation: That sentence is Tulving and Thomson's statement of the encoding specificity principle: retrieval succeeds when the cue matches what was actually stored at encoding. Context-dependent memory is a consequence of this principle, not the principle itself.

stem: Retroactive inhibition is a term of memory
explanation: Retroactive inhibition is new learning interfering with the retrieval of older material, so it is an interference account of forgetting, not a decay or delay account (decay says traces fade with time on their own).

stem: Having prior memory associations that make it difficult to form new memory associations is termed
explanation: Proactive inhibition is old learning interfering forward in time with new learning. Retroactive is the mirror image (new interferes with old), which is the trap here.
